criteria_results.title: Allegation 1: Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations â€” Identified
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output clearly identifies cherry-picking/favorable trade allocation as a distinct allegation category (Allegation Category 1). The output includes statistical analysis (t-statistic of 4.73, p<0.001), identifies the responsible parties (Whitmore and Chao), quantifies excess profits, and describes the harm to other clients.

criteria_results.title: Cherry-Picking: Correct Statutory Provisions Cited
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output explicitly cites both Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) of the Investment Advisers Act of 1940 in connection with the cherry-picking allegation. Specifically, paragraph 15 states: 'The Division alleges that during the Review Period, WCA, Whitmore, and Chao engaged in a pattern of favorable trade allocation commonly referred to as "cherry-picking," in violation of Sections 206(1) and 206(2) of the Advisers Act.' Additionally, paragraphs 70 and 71 confirm that both Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) are alleged to have been violated by the conduct described in Allegation Category 1 (Cherry-Picking Trade Allocations).

criteria_results.title: Scienter Distinction Between Section 206(1) and 206(2) Flagged
criteria_results.reasoning: The agent's output discusses Section 206(1) and Section 206(2) in several places, but it does not correctly identify the scienter distinction between the two provisions. The output states that Section 206(2) 'requires scienter (intent to defraud or recklessness)' and 'does not extend to conduct based on mere negligence,' which is actually incorrect as a legal matter (Section 206(2) is generally understood to require only negligence, not scienter). More critically, the output never flags the distinction between 206(1) requiring scienter and 206(2) requiring only negligence as a defense issue, nor does it note that the referral notice fails to delineate which specific conduct maps to which provision. The criterion specifically requires the output to identify that 206(1) requires scienter/intent while 206(2) requires only negligence, and to flag the referral's failure to map conduct to provisions as a defense strategy issue. The agent's output fails to do this correctly.

Under 'Obstructive Conduct During Examination,' the output notes that 'Allegation Category 6 (Obstructive Conduct) alleges that Whitmore, as a named individual respondent, directed deletion of trade allocation records during an active SEC examination, constituting direct evidence of obstruction under Section 209(e).' The output also cites Section 209(e) of the Advisers Act as the applicable provision. Whitmore's personal obstruction exposure is clearly and specifically addressed.
